title: Reza Hakimazar
text:
About
I am a master’s student in research psychology at the University of Hamburg, currently working under the supervision of Dr. Lennart Wittkuhn on developing Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable (FAIR) teaching materials.
My academic interests include social psychology, cultural psychology, and science communication. Recently, I have also been focusing on improving my skills in quantitative research methods in psychology. Here, you can listen to my podcast (it is in Persian).

title: CV
text:
University of Hamburg, Germany, Psychology, M.Sc. 2023 - present
University of Isfahan, Iran, Psychology, B.Sc. 2018 - 2023
Bachelor Thesis: The Association between Gender Discrimination and Life Satisfaction among Iranian Women: Mediating Role of Belief in a Just World
GPA: 18.78/20 in the Iranian System or 1.3 in the German System





Hamburg, Germany | September 2024 - present

This project is under the supervision of Dr. Lennart Wittkuhn at the University of Hamburg. The project aims to decentralize educational materials and make them more accessible for professors and students.




Hamburg, Germany | November 2024 - present

This course is provided by Dr. Lennart Wittkuhn at Erasmus University Rotterdam and University of Hamburg. The course aims to familiarize the students with applications of Git in psychological research.




Isfahan, Iran - Remote from Hamburg | October 2022 - present

Directed the remote organization of 3 scientific workshops, directly engaging with 100+ attendees and managing all communications
Published two issues of a psychology magazine for the public.
Implemented a feedback loop system to address customer inquiries, resulting in a 25 percent decrease in unresolved issues




Isfahan, Iran | February 2021 - September 2022

Collaborated with authors to write simple scientific reviews
Managed team of 10+ student editors and writers
Issued 7 volumes of scientific magazine




Isfahan, Iran | November 2018 - February 2021

Coordinated academic projects, liaising with professors, administration offices,and external entities to facilitate smooth project execution
Helped over 200 students to access better education quality
Organized 10+ education workshops





Academic Writing Course, University of Hamburg, Germany
Psychological Research Methods, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Iran
An Introduction to Statistical Analysis with SPSS, Negareh Institute, Tehran, Iran
EFPSA Research Summer School: The Importance of Understanding Diversity
July 2024, Szklarska Poręba, Poland European Federation of Psychology Students’ Associations (EFPSA) - Exploring how psychology actively strives to foster community cohesion and enhance the welfare of societies, communities, and individuals. In this project, my focus is on the Afghan population in Iran and Germany to investigate the interaction of social exclusion, perceived discrimination, and hate speech.



Software: Quarto, Git and Overleaf, Microsoft Office, Adobe Photoshop, Adobe Audition, Adobe InDesign Statistical Analysis: R programming and SPSS



Rajaei, S. N., Hakimazar, R., Mehrabi, H. (2021). Review Paper: Kidfluencing and Sharenting. First National Scientific Student Conference on Psychology, Ardabil.



English: Proficient, IELTS Certificate with an overall Band Score of 8 German: Advanced, TestDaF Certificate with an overall score of 19/20 Persian: Native



Top Student
I was among the top 15 percent of psychology students of Isfahan University at the time of graduation based on academic performance.



Accessibility Improvement Project, Field Researcher
I participated in a community initiative focused on improving street accessibility for people with disabilities. This project demanded detailed data gathering and interactions with municipal offices and public transport authorities to make meaningful changes.
Skinner Box Podcast, Host
This podcast consists of interviews and focuses on the experiences of people who study or have studied psychology.
Psycastic Podcast, Host
I hosted a 3-episode podcast about social psychology. The main point was to introduce the field of social psychology and its implications to a non-expert audience.

title: MORAL DECISION MAKING ACROSS CULTURES: INVESTIGATING THE EFFECTS OF STRESS
text:
Hello world!

I don’t know
sth to show









Han, Hyemin, Gary H. Glover, and Changwoo Jeong. 2014. “Cultural Influences on the Neural Correlate of Moral Decision Making Processes.” Behavioural Brain Research 259 (February): 215–28. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bbr.2013.11.012.


Hashim, Ismail Hussein. 2003. “Cultural and Gender Differences in Perceptions of Stressors and Coping Skills: A Study of Western and African College Students in China.” School Psychology International 24 (2): 182–203. https://doi.org/10.1177/0143034303024002004.


Jameson, Kimberly. 2005. “Culture and Cognition: What Is Universal about the Representation of Color Experience?” January. https://doi.org/10.1163/156853705774648527.


Ji, Li-Jun, and Suhui Yap. 2016. “Culture and Cognition.” Current Opinion in Psychology, Culture, 8 (April): 105–11. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.copsyc.2015.10.004.


Sachdeva, Sonya, Purnima Singh, and Douglas Medin. 2011. “Culture and the Quest for Universal Principles in Moral Reasoning.” International Journal of Psychology 46 (3): 161–76. https://doi.org/10.1080/00207594.2011.568486.


Seo, Minjae, Jinkyung Na, and Young-Hoon Kim. 2021. “Moral in Whose Eyes? Cross-Cultural Differences in Moral Decision Making and Behaviour.” International Journal of Psychology 56 (1): 175–82. https://doi.org/10.1002/ijop.12681.


Shapiro, Shauna L., Hooria Jazaieri, and Philippe R. Goldin. 2012. “Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction Effects on Moral Reasoning and Decision Making.” The Journal of Positive Psychology 7 (6): 504–15. https://doi.org/10.1080/17439760.2012.723732.


Singer, Nina, Monika Sommer, Katrin Döhnel, Sandra Zänkert, Stefan Wüst, and Brigitte M. Kudielka. 2017. “Acute Psychosocial Stress and Everyday Moral Decision-Making in Young Healthy Men: The Impact of Cortisol.” Hormones and Behavior 93 (July): 72–81. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.yhbeh.2017.05.002.


Starcke, Katrin, Christin Polzer, Oliver T. Wolf, and Matthias Brand. 2011. “Does Stress Alter Everyday Moral Decision-Making?” Psychoneuroendocrinology 36 (2): 210–19. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.psyneuen.2010.07.010.


Vallejo, Miguel A., Laura Vallejo-Slocker, Enrique G. Fernández-Abascal, and Guillermo Mañanes. 2018. “Determining Factors for Stress Perception Assessed with the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-4) in Spanish and Other European Samples.” Frontiers in Psychology 9 (January). https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2018.00037.

title: MORAL DECISION MAKING ACROSS CULTURES: INVESTIGATING THE EFFECTS OF STRESS
text:
Background


Stress is perceived and coped with differently across cultures (2003) and (2018).
However, there is little evidence about the effect of stress on moral decision-making across cultures.










Han, Hyemin, Gary H. Glover, and Changwoo Jeong. 2014. “Cultural Influences on the Neural Correlate of Moral Decision Making Processes.” Behavioural Brain Research 259 (February): 215–28. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bbr.2013.11.012.

Starcke, Katrin, Christin Polzer, Oliver T. Wolf, and Matthias Brand. 2011. “Does Stress Alter Everyday Moral Decision-Making?” Psychoneuroendocrinology 36 (2): 210–19. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.psyneuen.2010.07.010.


Vallejo, Miguel A., Laura Vallejo-Slocker, Enrique G. title: Reza Hakimazar
text:
Background


Culture has a sizable influence on cognition, from color perception to attention and moral decision-making (Jameson 2005), (Ji and Yap 2016), and (Sachdeva, Singh, and Medin 2011).
Previous studies on moral decision-making show significant cultural differences, even in neural representations (Han, Glover, and Jeong 2014) and (Seo, Na, and Kim 2021).
One major factor that affects moral decision-making is stress (Shapiro, Jazaieri, and Goldin 2012), (Singer et al. 2017), and (Starcke et al. 2011)





Background


Stress is perceived and coped with differently across cultures (Hashim 2003) and (Vallejo et al. 2018).
However, there is little evidence about the effect of stress on moral decision-making across cultures.
